refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary failover) with automatic switchover logic
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop redundancy and dynamic routing
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary cellular + secondary satellite link with intelligent link selection
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid dual-link architecture with primary cellular (LTE/5G) and secondary satellite (or long-range radio) communication paths, coupled with intelligent link selection and failover logic that automatically switches to secondary path upon primary degradation, and maintains link health monitoring with real-time availability metrics.

potential_sources: Link selection logic is decoupled from physical link implementations (cellular vs. satellite are independent DP realizations).
Failover logic is independent of link technology; it operates on link quality metrics.
Safety state transition (FR-NGHE-010.2) is decoupled from availability mechanism; it is triggered by link loss detection, not by the redundancy strategy itself.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits low coupling: the hybrid architecture allows independent optimization of each link path, and the failover logic is technology-agnostic. This supports future substitution of link technologies without redesign of the core availability mechanism.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Dual link infrastructure (cellular + satellite) is economically feasible and operationally acceptable to the customer.
ASSUMPTION: Link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss) are available from both link providers in real-time.
RISK: Satellite link latency may exceed operator tolerance for real-time control; mitigation: define acceptable latency bounds and test with operators.
RISK: Failover switching may introduce transient command loss; mitigation: implement command buffering and sequence numbering.
RISK: Dual link cost and power consumption may be prohibitive for some equipment classes; mitigation: offer tiered availability targets (0.99, 0.999, 0.9999) with corresponding link architectures.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) link selection algorithm (e.g., primary-preferred with automatic fallback on quality threshold breach), (2) failover latency budget (e.g., <500ms), (3) link health monitoring interval (e.g., heartbeat every 100ms), and (4) cost/power trade-offs for different link combinations. Consider adding a tertiary link (e.g., mesh radio) for ultra-high-availability scenarios (0.99999).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-link communication architecture, link selection logic, failover control, link health monitoring, and telemetry/tasking protocol handling. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services module (via SafetyAlert). It abstracts the underlying network infrastructure (cellular, satellite, radio) and presents a unified link availability interface to higher-level control logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from the tele-operator console or autonomous control system. Tasking is transmitted over the primary or secondary link depending on link selection logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics, availability status) back to the tele-operator console and monitoring systems. Telemetry is collected from the Vehicle module and forwarded over the active link.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits safety-critical alerts (link loss detected, failover triggered, safe state initiated) to the Safety Services module for logging, operator notification, and safety state enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with external network infrastructure (cellular provider, satellite provider, radio network). The Teleoperation module manages link selection, health monitoring, and failover across multiple Network Link instances.

module_containment_scores.notes: BEST FIT. Teleoperation module is explicitly designed for remote control communication. Dual-link architecture, failover logic, and link health monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Link selection and failover are entirely contained within this module. Minor diffusion: link quality metrics may be shared with Safety Services for safety state decisions, but this is a clean interface (SafetyAlert).

POOR FIT. While Safety Services owns safe state transitions upon link loss, it should NOT own the link availability mechanism itself. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would couple safety logic with communication infrastructure, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services should react to link loss events (via SafetyAlert), not manage the dual-link architecture.

POOR FIT. Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics, sensors, and actuators. Teleoperation link management is not a vehicle concern; it is a system-level communication concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter communication logic across the vehicle platform, making it difficult to upgrade or test independently.

POOR FIT. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it may coordinate between Teleoperation and Safety Services, it should not directly implement the dual-link architecture. This would bypass the Teleoperation module's purpose and create architectural ambiguity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of the dual-link availability mechanism because: (1) it already owns command-and-telemetry communication, (2) link selection and failover are intrinsic to teleoperation, (3) it has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and (4) it can be tested and evolved independently of vehicle and safety logic.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (dual-link logic + protocol handling + failover + monitoring), consider sub-dividing into Link Management (IM) and Protocol Handler (IM) sub-modules.

RISK: Link quality metrics must be reliably available from both network providers; if either provider fails to expose metrics, failover logic may degrade. Mitigation: define fallback heuristics (e.g., timeout-based failover if metrics unavailable).

RISK: Operator latency tolerance for satellite link may be unknown; if satellite failover introduces unacceptable delay, the hybrid architecture may not meet operational acceptance. Mitigation: conduct operator trials early to validate latency bounds.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm with Teleoperation module owner that dual-link management and failover logic are within scope. If Teleoperation module is already at capacity, consider creating a sub-module Link Manager (IM) within Teleoperation to isolate dual-link logic from protocol handling. Define clear ownership of link quality metric collection and failover decision logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-link with intelligent failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The dual-link redundancy and automatic failover are the primary mechanisms to achieve the target availability. The DP is specific, measurable, and testable. No ambiguity in the mapping.

diffusion_risks: MITIGATED: Failover logic is contained within Teleoperation module; no diffusion to Vehicle or Safety Services.
MITIGATED: Link quality metrics are passed to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface (clean boundary); Safety Services does not own link selection.
MITIGATED: Network Link interface is abstracted; Teleoperation module manages multiple Network Link instances without exposing complexity to other modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) for DP-D. The dual-link architecture, link selection, and failover logic are entirely owned by Teleoperation. External dependencies (Network Link, Vehicle, Safety Services) are accessed via well-defined interfaces. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Availability can be measured directly via link state monitoring and logging. Teleoperation module can expose link availability metrics (uptime, failover events, link quality) for verification. Acceptance test: run evaluation period, collect link state logs, calculate availability ratio, compare to ≥0.999 target.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, logging) are allocated to Teleoperation (link detection, redundancy, logging) and Safety Services (safe state transition). Allocation is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify link selection algorithm, failover latency budget, and health monitoring interval before detailed design.
Confirm with Teleoperation module owner that dual-link management is in scope; if not, escalate to system architect for scope clarification.
Define link quality metric thresholds (e.g., signal strength, latency, packet loss) that trigger failover; these thresholds should be validated with operators.
Plan operator trials to validate satellite link latency acceptability; if unacceptable, consider alternative secondary link (e.g., long-range radio).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, logging). DP-D is sound and well-contained in Teleoperation module, but further refinement is needed to address sub-FR details (e.g., failover latency, link selection algorithm, health monitoring). Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify the design and allocate sub-FRs to specific design parameters and modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds and trigger failover to secondary link.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition (e.g., equipment halt, operator alert) upon link loss detection.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain dual independent communication paths (primary cellular + secondary satellite/radio) with automatic link selection based on quality metrics.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log all link state transitions (up, down, failover, recovery) with timestamps for availability audit and root cause analysis.

revise.why: DP-D requires refinement to specify: (1) link selection algorithm (e.g., primary-preferred, quality-based switching), (2) failover latency budget (e.g., <500ms), (3) link health monitoring interval and metrics (e.g., heartbeat every 100ms, signal strength threshold), and (4) command buffering and sequence numbering to handle transient failover. These details are necessary for detailed design and verification.

assumptions: Dual link infrastructure (cellular + satellite) is economically and operationally feasible.
Link quality metrics are available in real-time from both network providers.
Operator latency tolerance for satellite link is acceptable (to be validated in trials).
Safe state transition logic (FR-NGHE-010.2) is owned by Safety Services and triggered by SafetyAlert from Teleoperation.
Link availability is measured over a continuous evaluation period (duration and start/end criteria to be defined by customer).
open_risks: Satellite link latency may exceed operator tolerance; mitigation: conduct operator trials early.
Failover switching may introduce transient command loss; mitigation: implement command buffering and sequence numbering.
Dual link cost and power consumption may be prohibitive; mitigation: offer tiered availability targets with corresponding architectures.
Link quality metric availability from network providers is uncertain; mitigation: define fallback heuristics (e.g., timeout-based failover).
Teleoperation module scope may be unclear; mitigation: confirm ownership with module owner before detailed design.
evidence_needed: Operator trials validating satellite link latency acceptability.
Network provider commitments for link quality metric exposure and failover support.
Cost and power analysis for dual-link architecture vs. single-link alternatives.
Detailed link selection algorithm specification and failover latency budget.
Safety analysis confirming safe state transition logic upon link loss.
Availability measurement methodology and evaluation period definition from customer.
